text: Iron supplementation has the greatest effect on physical performance in athletes with the lowest iron status, with benefits mostly limited to those who were originally in a deficit.
Iron supplementation can be beneficial for optimal erythropoietic response during altitude training, even in athletes with normal iron stores at baseline, but should be performed with caution.
Iron-deficiency anemia reduces physical performance in athletes by impairing oxygen transport and muscle function.
Alteration of the hepcidin response can affect the utilization of existing iron stores for erythropoiesis, influencing iron availability for performance.
Energy intake and carbohydrate availability may modulate the post-exercise hepcidin response, potentially affecting iron absorption and utilization.
Optimal vitamin D and B12 levels may contribute to improved iron status and help prevent anemia in athletes.
